data.title: Circle Internet Group: Earn A 22% Yield Selling Options On This Stablecoin Juggernaut
data.description:
A few months ago, we wrote an article titled "How We're Earning A 14%+ Yield Selling Puts On Circle Internet Group"

In it, we laid out a strategy for earning a significant yield from Circle Internet Group's (CRCL) recent IPO, by selling far out of the money put options that promised to pay out a 14% annualized cash yield.

While we thought - and still think - that CRCL will perform well over the long-term as a company, at the time, the stock was trading around $240 per share, which in our eyes was an extremely premium valuation.

In recent months, shares are down roughly 60% from highs, as market hype around stablecoins has dissipated and the company’s first earnings report showed rapidly deteriorating gross margins.

This has been a disaster for those who purchased shares during the post IPO rally.

However, the conservative trade idea that we recommended produced exactly the returns we thought it would, as Circle's stock held up above $80 per share through mid October:

Investors who took this trade pocketed $380 per contract - or a 4.5% return - in just 117 days, and didn't have to take assignment of the stock.

At present, Circle is in a slightly different position. Shares are much cheaper, volatility has fallen, and the company has announced a number of new initiatives that we expect will grow future revenues meaningfully.

That said, shares are still expensive, and the ongoing rate cut cycle we're seeing will likely present a significant revenue headwind in the short and medium term.

Thus, we’re still not quite willing to pay up for Circle's shares.

Instead, we're recommending a similar trade to the one we highlighted in June - that selling put options on CRCL will likely generate substantial yield with relatively minimal risk, especially versus holding the stock outright.

Today, we'll cover recent updates with CRCL, give our perspective on the upcoming earnings report, and highlight a 'short put' trading opportunity we think looks attractive.

Sound good? Let’s dive in.

The Thesis

To start, let’s cover our general thesis behind Circle, and why we think the company is poised to grow considerably over the next 5 to 10 years.

In short, we broadly, believe that an increasing share of transactions will move towards crypto markets over time. This is due to the fact that crypto markets provide near instant settlement, 24 hours a day, 365 days a year.

Not only that, but transaction fees are typically very low in Crypto, allowing for a near-frictionless exchange of value.

As decentralized payment networks propagate, we expect a larger share of payment volume, commercial volume, and even more complex financial transactions like trading and lending, to take place on public and corporate blockchains.

As use of these new money networks becomes more widespread, demand for stable value exchange will also grow.

This is where stablecoins fit into the current crypto ecosystem.

Sure, bitcoin is the 'gold standard', and is often thought of as digital gold, but the asset has too much volatility on a day-to-day basis to be used for transactions, treasury management, payment accounts, etc.

Stablecoins bridge the gap between Fiat currencies - which is what we’ve used in the past - to digital currencies, which is where we see the future of money heading.

Circle is the largest public issuer of stablecoins, which it primarily does through its USDC product which has almost 30% global market share.

The business is simple: as customers mint and use USDC, they leave their Fiat currency deposited with CRCL, who, in turn, invests into treasuries that produce significant reserve income.

This is CRCL's primary revenue source, and on a digital currency stock of tens of billions of dollars, CRCL produced over $2.1 billion in revenue over the last 12 months, almost all of which came from US treasuries.

Circle has three main levers that it uses to produce revenue.

First off, rates are key. If rates go down, then the return that Circle earns on its reserve holdings also decreases. Right now, this is a negative, but it also positions CRCL as a huge beneficiary in a rising rate environment.

Secondly, another one of CRCL's levers is the market cap of its overall outstanding stablecoins. This is an input that the company has much more control over, and thus, CRCL is invested in aggressively distributing and driving network effects for its three core financial products, USDC, EURC, and UYSC. CRCL expects a ~40% compounded annual growth rate for the overall stablecoin market, so if USDC can retain or grow market share then there’s a significant growth opportunity here.

Finally, CRCL offers value-add services on top of its digital money ecosystem. Many of the firm's recent innovations like the Circle Payment Network and Circle Gateway are primarily designed to boost adoption of USDC, but we expect that as the product ecosystem matures, CRCL will have significant leverage to charge additional fees for value add services, similar to what Visa (V) and MasterCard (MA) do right now.

Earnings

While we’re bullish on the company, CRCL's Q2 earnings numbers were mixed bag.

From an operational standpoint, the company announced it’s new public blockchain, Arc, and announced developments with the Circle Payment Network, Circle Gateway, Circle CCTP, and more. We expect these investments will pay off handsomely as the overall market share of stablecoins grows:

On the flip side, the company’s actual earnings came in much weaker than expected. The company reported $658 million in revenue - an all-time quarterly high (at least for the public numbers we have available) - but massive increases in the cost of revenues drove gross profit margins negative to the tune of $252 million in the Q2 alone.

Stacked on top of operating expenses of around $73 million for the quarter, CRCL produced negative operating income of $325 million. This massive increase in the cost of revenue represents continued investment in the distribution of CRCL's stablecoin network, including rewards paid out to deposit holders on platforms like Coinbase (COIN).

At the same time, the company has traditionally kept gross profit positive, at least for the four quarters preceding Q2. It’s clear that CRCL is allocating IPO proceeds towards growth, as opposed to improving profitability.

Broadly, this makes sense to us, but for investors looking at headline numbers, it obscures the story considerably.

Rates have also gone down over the last year, and so the linear increase we'd expect to see in CRCL's revenues, as a result of the increased Stablecoin market cap, has not been fully reflected.

This is the interest rate headwind we mentioned earlier.

Thankfully, Q3, which was just announced, showed a number of improvements. Analysts were estimating revenue of $700 million, with an EPS estimate of around $0.18, and CRCL managed to produce revenue of roughly $740 million and EPS of 0.82, which were both positive surprises.

Gross margins went up, which was a bit of a relief, but more broadly, we remain interested in the underlying 'operating' metrics like Meaningful Wallets, overall distribution, and market share. In our view, if the 'inputs' to the economic machine are positive, then sooner or later, the financial results will take care of themselves.

Meaningful Wallets grew up to 6.3 million, and we'd like to see this metric grow even more quickly in the future as network effects propagate. Similarly, we're happy with USDC's market share of roughly 30%, but this number will need to move north for CRCL to gain truly impressive economics:

At present, shares are trading at a P/S ratio of roughly 7.3x, but analysts expect revenues to grow to $6.2 billion by 2028, which should shrink this ratio considerably, to below 4x.

All told, we'd prefer to get shares a little bit lower than they’re currently trading, but we’re much happier with the value proposition in shares than we were six months ago, especially following the recent selloff.

The Trade

Our favorite way to play the stock is still by selling put options on shares.

Particularly, we like the idea of selling the February 19th, $65 strike put options:

Contracts are currently trading between $3.55 and $4.45 per contract, and we expect that you could get a contract filled at around $3.80, implying a $380 profit per contract if shares of CRCL stay above $65 over the next 99 days.

At this price investors stand to make 6.2% - or a little bit more than ~23% annualized - on their money, assuming CRCL options to stay above the $65 strike price.

In the event that shares trade below $65, option sellers still get the aforementioned yield, but they may also be assigned shares of Circle.

In that event, then investors can get shares of CRCL with a cost basis in the low 60s, which we think is a significant long-term value.

As a result, selling options on CRCL looks like a win-win trade - either you get a robust cash-on-cash return over the next few months, or you get a solid 'buy-the-dip' entry point into CRCL's long-term story.

Risks

Of course there are risks associated with this trade, and we'll point out three of those now.

First off CRCL has a number of revenue levers that we talked about earlier. Rates are a key risk for CRCL's long-term health, and if rates stay lower for longer, than it will have a serious impact on the company's overall profitability.

That would make shares considerably more expensive than they currently look, making an entry entry point at around $70 per share potentially less attractive.

Secondly, over the course of this option trade, we’re expecting an earnings report. Everyone knows that earnings are typically a volatile time for investors - so these events could send the stock materially lower. This has implications for short-term profitability for option sellers.

The IPO lock-up also expires this week, which is a consideration.

Finally, if you sell options in CRCL, then even if the stock's price goes to zero overnight, you’ll still be forced to purchase shares at $65 per share, representing a material capital impairment.

This profile is no different than holding shares outright, but it’s worth mentioning for those who haven’t traded options before.

Summary

Overall, we think selling put options on CRCL (again) is a great use of capital in today’s market. The 22%+ annualized yield represents a significant income to yield oriented investors, and a cheaper entry price point into CRCL's long-term story, in the event of assignment - looks like a strong value proposition.

As a result, we still rate shares a 'Hold', but we think selling $65 strike options could be a great use of your time and money.

Cheers~

data.title: Another Strong Year For CEFS, With Some Blemishes
data.description:
The Saba Closed-End Funds ETF (CEFS) has been one of my top income ETF for several years, owing to the fund's strongly successful strategy, strong performance track-record. In my articles on CEFS, I generally give a broad overview of the fund. In this one, I'll looking into some of the more recent developments of the fund.

CEFS's performance remains strong, with the fund seeing positive capital gains, distributing 7.4% in income to shareholders, and outperforming its benchmark. Outperformance has decreased though, with some small periods of underperformance. I previously identified this trend in April, and it has continued in the months since.

CEFS's distributions remain flat, and I don't foresee any distribution cuts in the coming months. Federal Reserve cuts are something of a headwind, especially in the medium-term. It is common for CEFS to pay a special distribution at the end of the year, which should bear the brunt of any cut.

The average discount of CEFS's underlying holdings has decreased, which somewhat reduces the income generated by the fund, and its potential returns.

Economic conditions have somewhat worsened, negatively impacting equity and high-yield bonds, which CEFS focuses on. Conditions are far from terrible, so I don't believe these to be significant issues right now, but conditions could always continue to worsen.

As should be clear from the above, recent developments are broadly discouraging for CEFS. I'm sticking with a buy rating due to CEFS's consistent success and outperformance, and because even though performance has definitely worsened, it remains strong and above-average. As economic conditions worsen, more risk-averse investors might prefer higher-quality investments.

CEFS - Quick Overview

A quick overview of the fund before looking at some recent developments.

CEFS is an actively-managed ETF focusing on income-producing closed-end funds. It is administered by Saba, a small investment management firm experienced with these funds. Saba is famous for launching activist campaigns on discounted CEFs, a strategy employed by/which benefits the fund itself too.

CEFS invests in a wide array of closed-end funds, currently focusing on equity funds, including those centered on covered calls.

CEFS currently yields 7.4%, excluding a special distribution in late December 2024. Said yield is significantly higher than the equity, bond, and high-yield bond average, lower than the 8.0% yield of the Invesco CEF Income Composite ETF (PCEF), its closest peer and benchmark. Accounting for special distributions, CEFS yields a bit more than PCEF.

CEFS's performance track-record is quite strong, with the fund significantly, consistently outperforming its benchmark PCEF since inception.

CEFS's outperformance was due to a broadly successful investment strategy, with several successful activist campaigns, several successful trades and savvy positioning. Leverage likely played a role too, especially in prior years.

With the above in mind, let's have a look at some of the more recent developments for the fund.

CEFS - Recent Developments

Continued, But Weaker, Outperformance

CEFS has significantly outperformed PCEF, its closest peer and benchmark, since inception. Outperformance is broadly consistent, and particularly strong since early 2022.

CEFS's outperformance was due to some combination of leverage and investment alpha / successful activist campaigns. Of particular note is the fund's activist campaigns, generally successful, generally profitable for the fund, its investors, and Saba's management. Looking at individual trades, the fund had a massive, short treasury position a couple of years ago, which almost certainly resulted in significant profits.

CEFS has continued to outperform YTD, but at a much-diminished rate. Returns remain strong, but they were stronger in the past.

I believe that a reduction / lack of activist campaigns explains some of the results above. Saba was quite aggressive last year with several BlackRock CEFs, leading to significant reductions in discounts for several CEFs, significant gains for the fund. I don't think there has been a campaign of that magnitude this year, at least not one that I am aware of. Due bear in mind, these issues are impossible for me to quantify.

Less successful individual trades are also a possibility. The short treasury position was likely responsible for significant gains before, but said position no longer exists, and being short treasuries hasn't been profitable for several years by now. Returns for the major bond sub-asset classes have been quite similar YTD, so CEFS hasn't missed any major trades here, there just simply haven't been any.

CEFS doesn't use leverage right now, which likely reduces returns and outperformance.

In most cases I wouldn't qualify the statement above with a likely, but because of CEFS's aggressive strategy, use of shorting and derivatives, I will. Sometimes leverage is due to accounting conventions, sometimes due to using derivatives or shorting, and sometimes part of a broader strategy which isn't that risky. CEFS matched the performance of its benchmark during 2020:

Outperformed during 2022:

Leverage did not lead to an increase in losses or drawdowns before, and I'm not convinced that it has magnified gains during bull markets either. It might have, and that seems likelier than not, but impossible to say for certain.

Distribution Analysis

CEFS has paid a monthly $0.140 distribution since a bit after inception, with no changes since mid-2017. It generally pays a special distribution in December, to meet ETF distribution regulatory requirements, and being paid of by capital gains / asset sales. Considering the strong total returns the fund has experienced YTD, another special distribution seems likely.

CEFS's share price has risen by 14.3% since inception, so distributions have not been destructive, nor caused NAV erosion. Due note that it is rare for ETF distributions to be destructive, but as CEFS focuses on closed-end funds, looking into this issue is important.

Discount Analysis

CEFS is an ETF, so generally trades close to NAV, with discounts and premiums being rare and almost always short-lived. Its underlying closed-end fund investments, however, do tend to trade with discounts, with these varying in magnitude. In general, CEF discounts have tightened these past few years, with lots of individual exceptions, as well as periods of (partial) reversal. Although I can't really analyze the movements of the discounts of CEFS's underlying investments, based on prior coverage, I believe these to be declining.

Right now, CEFS's investments have an average discount of 8.2%:

Compared to 9.1% in late 2024:

And 14.7% in late 2023:

Tighter discounts directly reduce the distribution yield of these investments, reducing CEFS's income and returns. Tighter discounts also somewhat reduce the possibility and magnitude of any capital gains from discounts normalizing.

As mentioned previously, CEFS sometimes engages in activist campaigns meant to close these discounts. Said campaigns are generally successful, and generally profitable. As fewer closed-end funds trade with sizable discounts right now, there are fewer opportunities for activist campaigns, which should mean less realized profit. I imagine some portion of CEFS's weaker performance is due to these issues.

Worsening Economic and Industry Conditions

Some of the more recent economic news has been negative, with worsening leading indicators, anemic job growth, and above-target inflation. Growth was strong this last quarter, negative the quarter before, somewhat weak YTD. Default rates seem either flat or slightly up, depending on the time period in question. Overall, conditions are worsening but are far from terrible.

Conditions could always worsen even more, perhaps enough to trigger / become a recession. Such a scenario would almost certainly cause losses across equity and high-yield fixed-income markets, causing significant pain for CEFS and its shareholders. Do bear in mind, a recession is far from guaranteed, and economic indicators do not seem negative enough to indicate one is likelier than not.

Credit spreads have also tightened these past few years and are nearing historical lows.

In my opinion, the above is a significant issue, and a highly certain one: spreads are tight, so risk-adjusted yields for high-yield bonds are weak. CEFS has some indirect exposure to high-yield bonds and so is negatively impacted by these issues. As it focuses on equities and has significant investments in other types of CEFs, the impact is much reduced.

Overall, conditions seem to be calling for a reduction in CEFS's performance. In practice, the massive equity bull market and tighter credit spreads have led to significant gains, although outperformance is much diminished.

Asset Allocations

CEFS invests in dozens of closed-end funds of different types, including those focused on equities, equity income, bonds, and diversified funds. CEFS has significantly reduced its bond exposure these past few years, with it going for around 50% of its portfolio in late 2023:

To around 23% as of today. Alternative and mixed funds have been the biggest beneficiaries, although figures do vary through time.

Said shift somewhat increases portfolio risk, volatility, and drawdowns. Considering CEFS's aggressive strategy, and the fact that many bond closed-end funds use significant leverage, I don't think that the impact here is incredibly significant, but there is definitely an impact.

I personally preferred the older allocations, as focusing on high-yield bond CEFs means more sustainable income, and because the track-record these funds is overall stronger. Because of the outstanding results of CEFS's strategy in the past, I expect the current allocations to work out well regardless.

Conclusion

CEFS is an actively-managed ETF focusing on income-producing closed-end funds. Although its performance has somewhat worsened in the past few months, it continues to perform quite well, seeing positive price gains, stable distributions, and slightly outperforming its benchmark. It continues to be a buy, although more risk-averse investors might prefer to focus on safer, higher-quality investments.

data.title: NewLake Capital Partners: REIT With Double-Digit Dividends, Nearly Zero Debt, And Significant Potential
data.description:
Introduction

Back when I first covered NewLake Capital Partners (OTCQX:NLCP), I called them a “Severely Undervalued REIT Paying A Double-Digit Dividend,” highlighting their significant expansion in a developing market, paying us massive sustainable dividends while waiting for a recovery.

With NLCP recently highlighting their readiness to diversify into other industries beyond cannabis-related real estate offering a potential catalyst for re-rating, on top of solid recent developments and a still sustainable dividend that now got to over 13%, this remains a very Strong Buy for me, recently opening a position.

Internal Developments

As we can see, the company still has 34 owned properties (31 of which are leased), with a weighted average yield of 13% on their leases, with 2.6% annual rent escalations and a weighted average of 12.3 years remaining on their lease terms.

As we can see, the AFFO reached $11.02 million in Q3 and $33.2 million in the first 9 months of the year, which is a ~1.4% increase YoY, which is not really amazing, but it comes at a time when some tenants reported issues that even led to vacancies. Still, they pay a ~13.31% dividend yield now that costs them about $35.96 million, placing them at an AFFO payout ratio of around 81.2% based on the annualized AFFO - in line with their target of 80% to 90% AFFO payout ratio. This massive dividend can also mean that buybacks can be attractive since they’d save on significant costs, so they should definitely consider them in my opinion. I’m surprised nobody even asked about this during the Q3 call.

However, during the Q3 Earnings Call, they also highlighted readiness to diversify beyond cannabis, which is an interesting development that I’d like to address:

I want to address a question I've been getting more frequently about whether we plan to diversify into other real estate sectors outside of cannabis. Our view is that it's our fiduciary responsibility to evaluate all avenues for creating long-term value for shareholders and that does include considering opportunities beyond cannabis. We continually monitor the broader real estate landscape to identify attractive investments, particularly where we can leverage our expertise in underwriting highly regulated special purpose properties. We'll continue to do that work. And if we find a compelling opportunity that aligns with our strategy and risk profile, we'll bring it to the Board for consideration.

This can also signal that the company either sees no significant opportunity in their market or that they see a benefit in diversifying and potentially improving their overall level of risk, which can even lead to a re-rating from the market. For reference, this comes as the company didn’t really invest that much in recent years, with barely any investing activity since the IPO, focusing more on stabilizing and adapting to the challenging environment instead of expanding.

Financially, based on NLCP’s latest report, we continue to see a very strong position, with the cash and cash equivalents alone being nearly enough to cover the company’s total liabilities and barely any debt - with the nearest maturity in May 2027. This leaves us with over $390 million in equity on a $270 million market cap today, so a very significant discount even if we use the depreciated asset value instead of the net asset value. And again, these assets can be repurposed in case cannabis turns out to be a failure, so it’s not like these assets would be worthless.

Still, I’ll mention again that due to their relatively very small number of properties, the company has significant tenant exposure, with nearly 50% of their rent coming from just 3 tenants. Their recent issues were caused by smaller tenants such as Revolutionary Clinics, AYR Wellness and Calypso going through restructurings/vacating properties/etc. over the last year, but just imagine what would happen if Curaleaf (24.1% of rent) files for bankruptcy, for example. NLCP owns the properties at the end of the day, but potentially missing on so much rent while also having to find a new tenant for so many properties (who would likely get an initial no-rent period) can instantly make the dividend unsustainable and cause significant issues for the stock.

About their tenants, they also provided some updates during the Q3 Earnings Release, while also offering a good summary of the broader industry:

Overall, our portfolio remains in a solid position. Tenants representing approximately 50% of our annual base rent reported solid third quarter results this week. Curaleaf expanded adjusted gross margins and generated over $100 million in cash flow year-to-date. Cresco delivered positive cash flow, reduced debt and completed a refinancing, while Trulieve delivered some of the highest gross margins in the industry, nearly 60% and generated over $60 million of free cash flow during the quarter. Green Thumb also reported another profitable quarter with $23 million of net income and $74 million of operating cash flow. That said, the broader cannabis landscape remains challenging in the absence of federal reforms, and we continue to focus on proactively managing risk and identifying opportunities to strengthen our portfolio.

Macro Developments

The main, major development that I have to mention especially for a REIT are US rate cuts, with a new one in October and hopes for another one in December. To summarize, this asset class depends on the difference between their cost of debt and the return they can get on their investments/leases, so when the debt premiums rise, their AFFO does the opposite - and that’s on top of higher interest expenses, weaker activity in the market due to higher cost of debt and pressure from smaller consumer discretionary budgets, as well as issues with the tenants, which we’ve seen recently. Although they’re nearly debt-free, these rates are still important since they affect the theoretically risk-free rate, which in turn affects their valuation, similar to many other REITs.

As mentioned before, NewLake also pointed out that there are significant advancements to keep an eye on, such as the recommendation to reschedule cannabis to Schedule III (from I) of the Controlled Substances Act, which they said would alleviate the tax burden on their tenants and estimating that their tenants would collectively save over $400 million annually from this. As of the time of writing this, the latest development is the DEA saying this process remains stalled despite some earlier commitments by the agency’s leadership and even the President’s to prioritize it.

For reference, Schedule III is significantly better than I, being defined as “moderate to low” potential for physical/psychological dependence; accepted medical use compared to high potential for abuse, no accepted medical use, and lack of accepted safety, coming with far leaner regulatory pressure compared to the current Schedule. As I mentioned in the Innovative Industrial Properties (IIPR) analysis (another Strong Buy), this also comes with better tax relief, allowing companies to deduct ordinary expenses such as rent, payroll, etc., so definitely good for the industry - although these improvements may still require further regulatory or legislative steps than this rescheduling.

This change can however lead to lower cap rates in the future since it would attract more players in the market and potentially even improve the credit ratings of tenants, but NLCP can also offset it through the volume they’d potentially gain, on top of the improved safety in the industry that can attract better valuations from the market. As a note, they still have a 12.3-year weighted average remaining lease term, so it will take a while to see these developments affect them.

However, this advancement can also be affected by the fact that the US Government just went through its longest shutdown in history, which also has an effect on the near-term future of rate cuts since the BLS also halted its data collection and publishing. As a result, the Fed has less access to up-to-date data, which comes at quite a bad time, with Powell even citing worries about unemployment and inflationary pressure and saying that a December cut is not clear yet.

Very recently, we've also seen Congress passing the large bill that also includes banning hemp-derived THC, basically rescheduling it to Schedule I like cannabis and closing a loophole created almost a decade ago by the 2018 Farm Bill. This is actually seen as a positive by regulated cannabis companies, who supported this ban, but it can be bad for companies including Curaleaf, who also has hemp-derived product lines. It could also be a step forward towards rescheduling them together to Schedule I eventually, but we'll see.

Still, the long-term likely remains in their favor, while potential markets such as Germany or the Czech Republic opening as this trend continues to develop. As they show, we’ve seen a massive increase in self-reported cannabis use in the US, with significant potential to expand in an industry that has significant access to already favorable and potentially improving regulations, with ~88% of US adults apparently supporting adult use and/or medical cannabis use.

Since I also covered IIPR - which is more than 5 times bigger in terms of market cap - I’d also like to mention the possibility of NLCP being an acquisition target, as their portfolio can be attractive especially if the cannabis rescheduling happens, offering a decent portfolio that comes at very solid rates, annual escalators and with a long average lease term. This can be a potential entry even for a giant REIT that would like to diversify and enter this market once it becomes better-regulated, still offering an opportunity even if cap rates were lower.

IIPR currently comes with a higher dividend (although I expect it to be cut if things don’t improve soon) and is also nearly debt-free, which is a massive advantage even if tenants continue to have issues. This makes sense for IIPR especially since they also highlight how IIPR comes at higher AFFO multiples, so the per-share accretion can be solid.

Updated Valuation

Starting with an annualized AFFO of $44.27 million, let’s assume a smaller growth rate this time and go for a 3.5% CAGR in the first 5 years thanks to rate cuts despite the industry’s growth potential, a 3% CAGR in the subsequent 5 and 2.5% terminal growth rate. I’ll now go with an even higher 12% WACC (significantly above my estimated ∼8.8%; assumptions: ~9% cost of equity, ~7% cost of debt) to account for their potential risks, plus the same 20.51 million shares outstanding and $15.97 million in net cash and $6.8 million in noncontrolling interests. Even what I’d call an extremely cautious assumption returns an enterprise value of $489.85 million, an equity value of $499 million and an intrinsic value of $24.33 per share, which is well above the current level.

Conclusions

NLCP remains a Strong Buy for me, with very solid fundamentals, significant financial flexibility with nearly zero debt, affordable double-digit yields, and huge potential for a recovery on top of industry tailwinds. Risks remain, of course, but the reward seems to be very much worth it, and I opened a position in the meantime - and am looking forward to adding on even better prices.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Pearl Diver Credit Company: Shares Are A 'Maybe,' Preferreds Are A 'Yes'
data.description:
Introduction

While I find the CLO market interesting, when it comes to CLO equity vehicles, I prefer to stick with securities that are somewhat higher up in the capital stack. Pearl Diver Credit Company (PDCC) focuses on CLO equity investments, but I’m mainly interested in the company’s preferred shares that have a mandatory redemption date. On top of that, those preferred securities are the only securities that rank above the common equity, which adds an additional layer of safety in case things go wrong on the CLO equity markets.

A look at the results

While the CEF still has to publish its financial results in the third quarter of the year, I wanted to take a step back and take a closer look at the H1 2025 results. As you can see below, PDCC reported total investment income of $11.5M, while its total expenses came in at almost exactly $5M, resulting in a net investment income of $6.5M.

Keep in mind that because the preferred dividends are considered an "interest expense," they're already included in the operating expenses rather than being recorded as a dividend. This means that the net investment income before taking preferred dividends into account exceeded $8M. This indeed means the preferred dividend coverage ratio was very strong at approximately 500%.

It's of course also always interesting to look at the balance sheet, as the CEF has to adhere to an asset coverage ratio of at least 200%. As the balance sheet below shows, this clearly wasn’t an issue, as the total asset coverage ratio came in at just under 400%, which, of course, is twice the required ratio.

Pearl Diver Credit currently pays a monthly distribution of $0.22 and I'm looking forward to seeing PDCC's Q3 results next week to determine for how much longer that distribution is sustainable.

The Series A preferred shares are my preferred investment

At the end of the first semester, Pearl Diver had 1.38M preferred shares outstanding, for just under $35M in preferred capital. The stock is trading with (PDPA) as its ticker symbol and offers a cumulative preferred dividend of 8% per year on the $25 principal value per preferred share. The preferred dividend is payable in monthly installments of $0.16667 per preferred share per month.

As you can see above, the preferred stock is currently trading at $25.24, representing a premium of 24 cents above the call price of $25/share. The preferred stock has a mandatory redemption at the end of December 2029 at a price of $25/share. This means the current yield to maturity is approximately 7.7%. However, Pearl Diver Credit is allowed to call these securities from Dec. 31, 2026, on. Although I think the odds of that happening are fairly low, the yield to call would be 7.1% in that case.

Investment thesis

The common shares of Pearl Diver Credit Company are attractive given they're trading close to the NAV and considering the distributions are still generous. I’m not keen on having any exposure to the common stock of CLO equity, but that doesn’t mean the stock can’t be attractive for investors with a higher risk tolerance level. That’s why I still label it a buy, but it should be considered a "speculative buy" and investors should be aware that lower interest rates on the financial markets will also reduce the NII and the ability to pay distributions going forward. I don’t consider that a "risk" as the distribution will likely evolve in tandem with market interest rates, and I mainly care about the spread between market rates and the distributions offered by an investment vehicle, even if that means the distribution (expressed as an absolute number) will decrease.

I am however much more interested in the preferred stock as both the yield to call as well as the yield to maturity are reasonable on a one-year or three-year basis. I currently have no position in any of the CEF’s securities, but the preferred shares are quite interesting and seem to offer a good risk/reward ratio.

data.title: BITO: I May Buy It To Monetize My Holdings, If Bitcoin Ever Reaches $1 Million
data.description:
I have been long Bitcoin (BTC-USD) for about five years, and I hold a Bitcoin position with an average purchasing price of ~$25,000. One of the reasons I like Bitcoin, among many others I discussed in my past coverage, is its extreme scarcity, making it a “superior” reserve asset from a technical standpoint.

At face value, a product like the ProShares Bitcoin ETF (BITO) defies the point of holding Bitcoin. Why force a yield on a “superior” asset?

In a world where Bitcoin matures as a global reserve, it doesn’t make logical sense to “sell” (more on how BITO works exactly soon) an asset you reasonably expect will increase in value forever (incidentally, this is also my argument for why Bitcoin will never become a global digital means of payment, but rather an asset).

Yet, this ETF exists, and it has $2.49 billion in AUM (Seeking Alpha data). Today, I ask myself what its purpose is and whether a case can be made to hold the BITO ETF.

How does BITO generate its yield?

The BITO ETF is described by its issuer as

the first ETF to target the performance of Bitcoin.

I find this definition odd. First, because the age of an ETF is hardly relevant per se, nor an argument to buy it in itself. That is especially true for BITO, considering that the fund does not invest in BTC directly. But, most importantly, because this description misses the main point of BITO, which is to generate an artificial yield from Bitcoin.

The main characteristics of BITO are as follows:

  • It does not invest directly in Bitcoin, but in futures and swaps tied to Bitcoin. The issuer markets this fact as “offering Investment Company Act protections and avoids direct bitcoin custody risk—unlike spot bitcoin ETFs.”

  • It distributes a monthly yield generated by futures-related gains. Its issuer specifies how: “the dividend distribution amount for the Fund may change significantly from month to month. In some months, the fund may not make any dividend distributions at all.”

  • It has a management fee of 0.95% and a Bid/Ask spread of 0.05%, which I find reasonable given the somewhat exotic nature of the fund.

BITO achieves its monthly yield primarily through a portfolio of front-month CME Bitcoin futures contracts that are continuously rolled forward, supplemented by OTC total-return swaps that replicate the same exposure.

The fund’s monthly distributions (a “yield” that stands at ~66% TTM according to Seeking Alpha) represent the net cash generated after expenses. These are derived from two principal sources:

  1. Interest income on the cash and short-term Treasury collateral posted for futures margin requirements

  2. Realized gains or losses from the rolling of futures contracts and the settlement of swaps.

When the Bitcoin futures curve is in backwardation (i.e., near-term contracts trade at a premium to further-out ones), rolling produces positive cash flow (sell high, buy low). In contango (further-out contracts trade above near-term or spot prices, the prevailing condition for most of Bitcoin’s history due to its appreciation), it generates negative roll yield (sell low, buy high).

In the case of BITO, a fund that has to keep rolling from one month to the next, contango slowly bleeds money for the fund (sell low, buy high every month), while backwardation prints money (sell high, buy low).

Combined with Bitcoin’s price volatility and fluctuations in short-term interest rates, these factors render the monthly payout inherently variable and unpredictable. Distributions are therefore not fixed income but rather opportunistic cash flows, which may sometimes be return of capital rather than true yield.

What’s the point of holding BITO?

Back in April 2024, I wrote an article on BITO arguing how one of the main reasons to hold this fund had to do with the fact there were options available for this ETF. That thesis is now invalid, as there are options available in the market for spot Bitcoin ETFs, such as the iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (IBIT).

The point of holding BITO, today, seems to be solely its yield. But, as I outlined at the beginning of this article, why would a Bitcoin bull want to sell their Bitcoin to generate an artificial, somewhat unpredictable yield? The more logical approach would be to take a margin loan against Bitcoin, or simply hold it and sell parts of a BTC position when needed.

However, investing in the real world can be a little bit different from investment theory. That’s the case, for example, of why dividend investing is successful and pursued by many, even as financial theory suggests dividends should be completely irrelevant in investing.

In the case of BITO, I think a reasonable use case could concern someone who has held Bitcoin for a long time and wants to generate a yield out of their BTC stake to pay living expenses, without completely liquidating the underlying asset or having to worry about corrections.

That use case, however, hardly exists for anyone at this point in time, in my view. Bitcoin, today, remains a high-stakes bet on this asset maturing as a global reserve. Owning BITO would mean certain underperformance against holding BTC, both as a result of its hefty management fees as well as taxable events (in the form of its yield being paid off).

In some very niche cases, BITO could still make sense. For example, early Bitcoin investors who find themselves having significant amounts of BTC and wanting to “monetize” them. Holding BITO, for such people, would make particular sense if they were bullish on Bitcoin. That’s because the BITO fund performs best in backwardation scenarios (as I described previously). Were Bitcoin to keep appreciating roughly in line with its past, investors in BITO would benefit from a relatively safe yield.

However, even for those specific use cases, there are some limits. Namely, the fact that BITO’s yield is unpredictable and may be too high for the needs of most.

Overall, I think this is a fund that was launched with very different intentions, at a time when investors could not find almost any Bitcoin-related investment in the markets. Today, with a vast choice of spot Bitcoin ETFs in the market (which include options), the BITO ETF has a niche use case.

Risks and tax considerations

I have already covered the limits of the BITO ETF throughout this article. These include its hefty fees, artificial and fluctuating yield, and the fact the fund is not directly invested in Bitcoin. Beyond the technicalities of BITO, there are also some obvious broader risks in investing in a financial tool tied to Bitcoin.

Bitcoin’s technical superiority (such as fixed supply, decentralization, and censorship resistance) does not, by itself, guarantee that it will ever achieve global reserve asset status. Investors who fully understand these properties can still be rationally bearish on Bitcoin. In other words, one can view Bitcoin as a legitimate hard asset and simultaneously doubt that it will ever seriously rival gold, the dollar, or other sovereign reserves.

The core risk of owning Bitcoin today is precisely this: that it remains a speculative digital asset rather than maturing into the neutral, apolitical store of value.

For BITO specifically, a further element to be considered relates to the tax implications of holding such a fund. With a hefty monthly yield, most investors will find themselves in the situation of having to pay significant income or dividend tax. I am not a tax expert, nor am I based in the US, but investors should carefully consider whether such a yield, with its taxable events, makes sense for their objectives.

Conclusion: I may buy BITO to “monetize” my Bitcoin stash, but not today

As a Bitcoin bull, I do not see myself holding an instrument like BITO for the foreseeable future. I would consider it only if Bitcoin were to mature into a global reserve asset. As per my BTC bull case, this would mean Bitcoin matching gold in market capitalization and becoming worth up to $1,000,000 per coin.

If that were to happen, I think I could see a role for a small position in BITO to monetize my Bitcoin stash. Exactly as I favor dividend investing to some extent, an artificial yield could be desirable to cover living expenses and not having to think about when to sell.

My “HOLD” rating of BITO reflects its niche use cases today, and not my rather bullish outlook on Bitcoin. I think investors who have BITO in their portfolio are likely to desire an artificial yield on Bitcoin. If that is the case, and investors are OK with the fund’s fluctuating yield and its tax implications, I see no issue in having BITO in one’s portfolio.

Investors who want to be broadly exposed to Bitcoin or do not need a yield, on the other hand, would be better off rotating into something like the IBIT ETF.

data.title: Google: Holding A High Market Share In Search
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Baron Fifth Avenue Growth Fund Q3 2025 Shareholder Letter.

Baron Fifth Avenue Growth Fund continued adding to the parent company of Google, the world’s largest search and online advertising company, Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL). While the company continues to hold high market share in search, it also owns the world’s leading video platform, YouTube, a leading hyperscaler, Google Cloud, a leading ad network, and optionality in a number of smaller subsidiaries like the autonomous vehicle company, Waymo.

Generative AI models like ChatGPT have introduced a bear case for Alphabet for the first time in over 20 years, centering on the potential disruption of its core search business and massive advertising revenue stream. While Google has long held a near-monopolistic position in the search engine market, the rise of LLMs like ChatGPT could lead to a shift in user behavior and a potential erosion of Google’s long-standing lead. For long-term investors, the central risk is that generative AI-powered interfaces could fundamentally change how users seek information, bypassing traditional search results and threatening Alphabet’s most profitable advertising engine. However, Google has thus far only seen growth in the number of search queries supported by AI overviews and AI mode, which have also expanded the addressable opportunity to longer and more complex queries. Additionally, Google has seen paid clicks reaccelerate despite the rise of ChatGPT.

More broadly, the bull case for Alphabet is multifaceted, leveraging its extensive AI expertise and vast ecosystem. Firstly, Alphabet’s DeepMind is a leader in AI research, with a superior talent pipeline and retention, allowing the company to attract top-tier talent and convert research into products at scale – see the recent Veo 3 video generation model release or the significant progress that Alphabet has made with its Gemini models. Second, in contrast to rivals like Meta, Alphabet’s diversified revenue streams and hardware investments, such as its custom tensor processing units, provide a crucial advantage. By developing its own full-stack AI infrastructure, from hardware to applications, Alphabet gains superior price-to-performance relative to general-purpose chips like GPUs. This approach enables a more cost-efficient scaling of AI workloads all else being equal. Third, Alphabet’s scaled user aggregation surfaces, offers a significant competitive moat. The company has a “billion user club,” with nine software products boasting over a billion users each, including Android, Chrome, YouTube, Gmail, and Google Maps. This massive, existing user base provides a substantial advantage by effectively lowering customer acquisition costs for new AI products. Critically, this scaled user base is a powerful magnet for developers, who are incentivized to build applications that integrate with Google’s AI engine to reach a vast audience. This flywheel effect – where users attract developers, and a robust app ecosystem attracts more users – strengthens Alphabet’s position at the heart of the AI landscape. A fourth forward-looking driver is the coming evolution in commerce, in which every piece of content becomes “shoppable.” With its scaled services like YouTube and Google Maps, combined with advanced AI, Alphabet is uniquely positioned to capitalize on this shift. For instance, AI models could recognize products in real-time within videos or images, and via integrations with merchants, enable them to be shoppable just in time – this should strengthen Alphabet’s positioning in commerce.

Lastly, the recent resolution in the Department of Justice case against Alphabet, which was close to a best-case scenario, removes a significant overhang from the stock. Getting everything Alphabet has to offer across its platform and ecosystem at a 23 times P/E multiple is attractive for long-term investors in our view.

Investors should consider the investment objectives, risks, and charges and expenses of the investment carefully before investing. The prospectus and summary prospectus contain this and other information about the Funds. You may obtain them from the Funds’ distributor, Baron Capital, Inc., by calling 1-800-99-BARON or visiting BaronCapitalGroup.com. Please read them carefully before investing.

Risks: The Fund invests primarily in large cap equity securities which are subject to price fluctuations in the stock market. Even though the Fund is diversified, it may establish significant positions where the Adviser has the greatest conviction. This could increase volatility of the Fund’s returns.

The Fund may not achieve its objectives. Portfolio holdings are subject to change. Current and future portfolio holdings are subject to risk.

The discussions of the companies herein are not intended as advice to any person regarding the advisability of investing in any particular security. The views expressed in this report reflect those of the respective portfolio managers only through the end of the period stated in this report. The portfolio manager’s views are not intended as recommendations or investment advice to any person reading this report and are subject to change at any time based on market and other conditions and Baron has no obligation to update them.

This report does not constitute an offer to sell or a solicitation of any offer to buy securities of Baron Fifth Avenue Growth Fund by anyone in any jurisdiction where it would be unlawful under the laws of that jurisdiction to make such offer or solicitation.

Alpha measures the difference between a fund’s actual returns and its expected performance, given its level of risk as measured by beta. Beta explains common variation in stock returns due to different stock sensitivities to market or systematic risk that cannot be explained by the US Country factor. Positive exposure indicates high beta stock. Negative exposure indicates low beta stock. Price/Earnings Ratio or P/E (next 12 months): is a valuation ratio of a company’s current share price compared to its mean forecasted 4-quarter sum earnings per share over the next twelve months. If a company’s EPS estimate is negative, it is excluded from the portfolio-level calculation. EPS Growth Rate (3-5-year forecast) indicates the long term forecasted EPS growth of the companies in the portfolio, calculated using the weighted average of the available 3-to-5 year forecasted growth rates for each of the stocks in the portfolio provided by FactSet Estimates. The EPS Growth rate does not forecast the Fund’s performance.

BAMCO, Inc. is an investment adviser registered with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC). Baron Capital, Inc. is a broker-dealer registered with the SEC and member of the Financial Industry Regulatory Authority, Inc. (FINRA).

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Harrison Schwartz: Why PPLT Is My Top Platinum ETF Pick Through 2030
data.description:
Follow Harrison Schwartz on Seeking Alpha!

Follow PPLT on Seeking Alpha!

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Content is offered for information purposes only. Unless stated otherwise, any and all individuals participating in the video are third parties that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body. Unless stated otherwise, the views or opinions expressed may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole.

The accuracy and completeness of content shared cannot be guaranteed. Seeking Alpha does not take account of your objectives or financial situation and does not offer any personalized investment advice. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker, US investment adviser, or investment bank.

Daniel Snyder: Hey everyone. Welcome back to another episode of ETF Spotlight. I'm Daniel Snyder from Seeking Alpha. Today, we are lucky enough to be talking to Harrison Schwartz. He is going to take us into the world of platinum and palladium and what his opinions are in the space right now.

Obviously, lots of focus on gold and silver and those commodities through the years, specifically more recently with a lot of worry out there. Maybe it's inflation, maybe it's debt, maybe it's government shutdown, whatever it might be, right? But platinum and palladium have been these silent lurkers over the years, and we're excited to jump into the conversation with him. So, obviously, we're going to jump into an ETF.

And today, that will be PPLT. That's abrdn’s Physical Platinum Shares ETF. And, Harrison, I would love to hear the case of one, first, you've been writing on Seeking Alpha since 2018. How did you get into specifically these metals?

Harrison Schwartz: So, I've always, for me, it's always been about how do we defend the portfolio? How do we make a portfolio that's robust against any situation that – any all-weather. Right? So, I always like alternatives. I'm always looking for alternatives because you get low correlations, and if you have a lot of things with low correlations, that lowers portfolio volatility.

Platinum, for me, is great because it has relatively low correlations, but it gives you exposure to hedges against monetary risk. So, dollar devaluation hedge, like gold, but under the radar. And I think that's really valuable because you get some more potential for alpha because it's not seen by most people. And so, I look mostly for alternatives. I think platinum and palladium are very interesting for those reasons.

DS: Well, so let's talk about this. Because I know most people think about let's take gold, for example, we just mentioned gold. There's demand from central banks from around the world. Investors can understand, okay, there's the buyer, there's the constant demand. Where does the demand come from for platinum and palladium? What's the end product, or what's the usage, or what's the store of value, and how does that market shape out?

HS: Platinum is interesting, because it's much like silver, where you get a mix of industrial demand and investment demand. There's not that central bank demand, which is something that maybe we'll see change as the precious metals boom goes on, but we have a mixture of industrial demand, more industrial demand with platinum than we see with silver. So that gives you some exposure to the positive and negatives of the economy while also having a big hedge against inflation. Because ultimately, if things really drive up, all the precious metals will go up probably similarly, not just gold.

DS: Alright. So, what about, let's talk about the supply aspect of it, right? We know how hard it is to mine gold and it adds to the source of value there, but is platinum and palladium, are those easier to mine? Where are they mined? What's the backdrop of the geography?

HS: So platinum, what's interesting is, it's somewhere around a 30th as much abundant as gold. So, there's far less platinum in the ground than gold. It’s far less produced every year than gold. And it's mostly produced in South Africa. It's mostly produced by one company, Sibanye-Stillwater. So, that gives you, one, if anything happens to these few mines in the world that produce it, that could mess up supply. And we're seeing that right now where the company, Sibanye-Stillwater, is cutting production because of lower prices, and that alone is enough to probably drive up a shortage of the metal. And on top of rising investment demand, I think that gives you a good catalyst for a long-term breakout of sustained shortage even if industrial demand does not keep up.

DS: So, you mentioned Sibanye-Stillwater Limited, that's ticker symbol SBSW. Are they like the power player of the market? Are they the OPEC PLUS of this realm?

HS: They really are because you get – the specific numbers I think people might want to look up, but I think it's around a quarter of all production comes from this one company. So, they have a huge amount of movement in the market for both platinum and palladium. And South Africa in general is very concentrated in that market. So, if you see tariffs, if you see any trade issues, all of a sudden, how are we going to get this metal? And where else does it come from? Russia. So, it's not seen in great abundance outside of countries where the trade stability is a little bit less strong than we usually see with other metals.

DS: Now, you had mentioned that the Platinum ETF, PPLT is actually one of your top investment picks all the way up to 2030. Why do you have the conviction to go that far in advance? I mean, it's almost just only 2026 right now, right? So, you're talking about multiyear call here.

HS: Yeah. And, truthfully, my bullish conviction has not changed much over the past five years or however long I've been looking at this. Because, to me, as an investor, I always want to look long-term. I always want to think, like, I think you can make the most alpha by thinking 10 to 20 years in the future. So, 10 years in the future, we know that there's going to be some difficulty with financing the government. We're seeing more dovish monetary policies or quick cuts of interest rates, things like that, which could definitely push inflation a bit higher than we've seen over the past 20 years or past 40 years, really.

And so, I think long-term I expect higher demand for investments for inflation hedges. Platinum, I think, is a really good pick for your retail investor, not necessarily your central bank. So, I think there's a great deal of upside there.

DS: Now, what do you think about, you had added this great chart in your last article on this ETF. A comparison between the gold price and platinum price, right? And if I just look at the chart, I can see, like, there's a massive spike back during the GFC and then a massive dip and, like, crazy volatility all the way, it looks up until, like, 2011, 2012, 2013 maybe. And then a steady pullback over the years where we haven't even surpassed that all-time high. So, do you feel like we're going back to all-time highs? Are we going back and then soaring above them? Are we going to go back and consolidate there? What are your thoughts?

HS: So, truthfully, I think the road back to that parity ratio we saw in the 2000s, may be slow because since then there's been more EVs, less demand for diesel vehicles. So, that lowers industrial demand in the short-term. I think that we're seeing more industrial demand growth from medical and other, like, hydrogen fuel cells, other technologies that we're seeing industrial demand level off and stabilize, not grow sharply.

I really think the growth is going to be from the investment side, and that remains speculative because that depends on having more monetary volatility that we've seen over the past year. Though I think that's likely.

DS: So, your case, if I understand, and please correct me if I'm wrong, is, the price of platinum, palladium will go up because it will be a store of value similar to gold, right, which people traditionally flock to. Nowadays, we also have Bitcoin as well, right? So, do you feel like, I mean, is there a case, it seems like people want to consolidate into one, maybe two sources of value in times of trouble. I mean, why do we start diversifying three, four, five? And then, do you get what I'm saying, where if you lose the consolidation, you might lose the additional value on top of it?

HS: Well, the way I'd see it is, we know a lot of companies need platinum for industrial uses. So, inflation rises, the cost of producing it will rise, as we've seen. And if the cost of producing it rises, then less will be produced if the price doesn't rise too. So, I think that there's no reason to say, we need to go on one thing only. Anything that we're seeing demand is existing and the cost of production is rising with inflation, with global inflation, that will ultimately push things up.

Whether or not, gold, I can see – gold should move first, because gold is the most robust hedge against inflation, but your other metals, like platinum, palladium, even copper, all in the long-term should rise according to their cost of production. And really, you're hedging an increase in cost of production primarily.

DS: Alright. So, let's talk to investors real quick before we start to wrap things up here. You had mentioned, this is mainly found in Russia. It's mainly found in South Africa. Like, to me, that sounds risky, right? Is that the biggest risk for this space, or are there other risks that you also think about in the back of your mind?

HS: Bullish or bearish risks?

DS: Let's go with both.

HS: Okay. So, I definitely think on the supply side, absolutely, there's that trade risk and production risk with either of those two areas. And that's bullish as an investor because that supply is, and it seems like the strategic metals or rare earth metals, once you don't have that supply, all of a sudden, those prices go right up. So, that's a, supply side risk is bullish. Risk - so, of course, if we see a change in policy and fiscal monetary policy that, is really strong to defend the U.S. dollar and push inflation down, that would moderate the trend in precious metals. I don't think that's likely, but that's where I would say is the greatest risk in all precious metals right now, especially because we've seen prices rise so much in gold.

DS: Alright. So, let me ask you this. I'm looking at PPLT here, the ETF on Seeking Alpha. And I noticed that really the price increase started around May 20th, beginning June this year. What has been happening since that moment? Because tariffs were in April. Is that when South Africa and tariffs dispute happened? What was the event there where this started to increase? I think it's up 71% year to date.

HS: I think the market is realizing that there is a supply shortage in the short-term and probably the long-term. We've seen Sibanye-Stillwater production cuts for platinum and palladium due to low prices, primarily for palladium, but that lowers production for platinum because they're mined together.

So, I think that that alone is driving higher prices, and then we're seeing more market realization of this as a long-term trend on top of higher investment demand. Gold soared since last year, and now people are saying, oh, hey, what else can I get into that might have similar profile to gold? Platinum's a great choice. Decent choice. I would say it's a great choice with more industrial risk.

DS: Well, so let's go with that. Great choice versus decent choice. It also leads me to think about how much of a portfolio allocation should be within this space. So, what would you say? I mean, is this like a 5% BlackRock - , Crypto, 5%, like, 5%, 10%, 15%? Or you're you saying let's swing the bat and let's go 30%, 35%?

HS: The one thing I'd say is, the cost of production of platinum is very close to its current price. So, for me, the long-term downside risk is pretty low. I'll be surprised to see prices go lower than they had been last year. So that gives you low risk that could allow for a higher portfolio position. For me, I would keep the lower portfolio position diversifying a few metals. This is, I think, the largest metal position I would have because I think it's undervalued, compared to the other metals, but, yeah, 5%, 15%, probably no more than 15%.

DS: Alright, Harrison. Let's go ahead and wrap it up there. Everyone, PPLT, this is the platinum and palladium side of things. Obviously, go follow Harrison Schwartz here on Seeking Alpha. Harrison, I hope you continue to write about this space. I find it fascinating. I know many others do as well. Thank you for joining the program this week. Everyone, this has been another excellent episode of ETF Spotlight, and we'll see you again here next week. Take care.

Follow Harrison Schwartz on Seeking Alpha!

Follow PPLT on Seeking Alpha!

data.title: Nvidia Stock Forecast: The Path To $6 Trillion
data.description:
Two years ago, Nvidia Corporation's (NVDA) April 2023 quarter delivered a historic 18% beat, followed by an even bigger 30% beat in July 2023. Compare that to the most recent quarter ending July 2025 — just a 4% beat, the smallest in two years. The narrowing beats combined with a rare QoQ decline in the Compute segment could be interpreted as Nvidia is running out of steam.

I am frequently asked by notable media anchors if Nvidia could be topping, and as you’ll see below, I substantiate why Nvidia is not running out of steam. I spoke with Charles Payne from Fox Business and Caroline Hyde from Bloomberg, followed by a 45-minute interview on the topic with Maggie Lake of Wealthion. In these conversations, we discussed some of the most pressing issues that AI investors face – does China revenue matter for Nvidia, how does the world’s most valuable company continue to grow, are we in an AI bubble, and lastly – where is we are positioning next.

For those new to my firm, the Tech Insider Network is among the top-performing AI portfolios globally. Our track record was built in part by taking a significant Nvidia position years before the market embraced the AI story, with allocations as high as 10–20%. While our calls on Nvidia are well recognized, we’ve also held a number of lesser-known AI winners that together have driven a cumulative return of 210%. To put that in perspective, if we were a hedge fund, we’d rank #2 — and if we were an ETF, we’d rank #5.

What that ranking should communicate to you is that the Tech Insider Network does not rest on our laurels, we rigorously pursue a portfolio positioning that will outperform. Let me rephrase this to say that my firm will not continue to hold Nvidia out of fandom, rather, we will continue to hold Nvidia only if we believe the stock continues to offer alpha.

Below, I explain why this stock has the potential to outperform the indexes — and how we’re positioning to capture even greater returns from lesser-known stocks that are riding Nvidia’s momentum.

Is There Alpha Left In Nvidia Stock?

After being crowned the world’s most valuable company with a market cap of over $4 trillion, it's natural to wonder if the easy money has already been made. For investors, the debate comes down to whether Nvidia can keep outpacing both the Nasdaq and its semiconductor peers, given its phenomenal run.

Regardless of market fluctuations, Nvidia’s product road map is not slowing down. As described in the videos below, Nvidia is on “the eve of releasing its next generation of GPUs,” combined with its enviable software platform CUDA and strong QoQ growth in AI networking (NVLink, Spectrum-X, InfiniBand).

In the interview below with Charles Payne of Fox Business, which took place ahead of earnings, I offer key reasons that Nvidia continues to offer alpha as we head into calendar year 2026 and why we plan to buy the stock on any dips.

I also had the opportunity to talk to Maggie Lake of Wealthion on the importance of Nvidia and why the company is moving from its 2.0 era to the 3.0 era, and how this transition from server-scale to a rack-scale AI systems company is key as to why Nvidia has further room to run. In the clip, I also describe the opportunity that Nvidia is poised to capture that will be as large as the AI hardware opportunity.

The AI Bubble: Fact Or Fiction

Fears as to whether AI is reaching bubble territory were ignited this past month when OpenAI (OPENAI) CEO Sam Altman compared AI to the dot-com era, stating:

“When bubbles happen, smart people get overexcited about a kernel of truth. If you look at most of the bubbles in history, like the tech bubble, there was a real thing. Tech was really important. The internet was a really big deal. People got overexcited.”

Given that OpenAI is seeing about $20 billion in revenue as of now with no profits, it makes sense that Altman would be concerned about valuations. However, a lack of profits is certainly not Nvidia’s concern. The company had a GAAP operating margin of 60.8% in Q2 and an adjusted operating margin of 64.5% with over $13 billion in cash flow for the quarter.

There is clearly a bifurcation in the AI economy, as some companies must spend heavily to compete, while others are profiting heavily from the steep competition.

In as lucid of a manner as possible, I break down in this video the following:

  • Why technically every tech trend goes through a boom/bust, why this should not scare investors off, plus the strategy investors can use to successfully win with surging tech trends.
  • What companies are more insulated from the effects of a bubble, and why AI software like OpenAI is at higher risk.
  • The differences between an enterprise technology and a consumer technology in terms of how AI will repay Big Tech capex.

Nvidia's Stock Can Reach $6 Trillion Market Cap By Next Year

In an interview that went viral with Caroline Hyde of Bloomberg, I discussed why it’s important to not focus too closely on quarterly earnings reports for determining Nvidia’s growth potential, and to rather focus on GPU generations.

If we look at the revenue potential for quarterly data center revenue once Blackwell and Blackwell Ultra ship in volume, this leads us to a $6 trillion valuation by next year.

Predicting 50% upside may not seem like much given Nvidia is up 325% since early 2024 and up 4,000%+ since my firm first bought the stock in 2018, however, there is a chance investors have an opportunity to get Nvidia lower.

For example, as recent as January of this year, my firm predicted Nvidia would dip below $100 – this was published before the DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) news hit. This afforded our followers a better entry, leading to higher upside. Next week, we will be updating our buy plan for Nvidia for free.

The Inevitable AI Trend That Will Profit Most from Nvidia's Upcoming Generation Of GPUs

Nvidia 1.0 was defined by the company's lead in gaming, whereas Nvidia 2.0 was defined by the introduction of Tensor Cores with Volta. These cores evolved through Ampere and the Hopper generation, ultimately leading to the transformer engine and more precision support for accelerating AI compute. The market eventually caught onto the enormity of the AI market as Hopper facilitated more accurate GenAI applications coming to market. You can read more about this history in this analysis: “Here’s Why Nvidia Will Reach a $10 Trillion Market Cap by 2030.”

Looking forward, what I am dubbing as “Nvidia 3.0” will be the shift from 8-GPU server-scale systems to 72-GPU rack-scale systems — which is augmented with the back-to-back release of Blackwell and Blackwell Ultra. Nvidia’s new rack-scale systems will mark one of the most significant turning points in its history. Regardless of what market participants think about Big Tech’s surging capex spend, the spend will continue to rise as those who get the NVL72 systems (that are beginning to ramp now) will have a critical advantage over those still on the HGX or DGX systems with 8 GPUs.

There is one inevitable trend that will profit most from the upcoming generation of GPUs, with significant momentum from Nvidia’s new rack-system architectures. This 5X to 9X opportunity that is already beginning to take shape with material evidence of the momentum.

Don’t miss out on what a leading AI portfolio and analyst team is saying about where to position next to take advantage of AI’s next major move.

AI Networking Is The #1 Opportunity From Nvidia's Upcoming GPUs

Under the hood, what defines the upcoming generation of GPUs from the previous generation is AI networking. When you move from 8 GPU severs to 72 GPU rack-scale systems, your intra-rack networking shifts, as does the rack-to-rack for larger clusters due to higher GPU density. For example, we’ve seen hyperscalers plan to deploy 1 million GPU clusters by 2027-2028, up from clusters with 10s of thousands right now. Therefore, both scale-up and scale-out architecture are going through a rapid transformation on the networking side.

In this video, I discuss why the Tech Insider Network is concentrated in AI networking stocks as our #1 trend right now:

Conclusion:

Nvidia and its ecosystem of suppliers are the best way to position for the rest of 2025. While headlines were fixated on China, the bigger story is clear: H20s contributed about $4B, while Blackwell generated an estimated $28B this past quarter — over a $100B run rate. This is why my stance has been to focus on GPU generations to track the AI opportunity rather than quarterly earnings or any single geographic area for seven years and counting. Semiconductors are particularly tricky, as the cyclical bottom can often be the best time to buy.

Keep an eye out for the Tech Insider Network's new buy plan for Nvidia, hitting inboxes next week. Our last buy plan provided an entry under $100 when the market felt the stock was invincible. This led to entries with real-time trade alerts at $94.48 and $87.99, with real-time trade alerts sent to our Premium Members.

For 2025, the Tech Insider Network has worked to identify key Nvidia suppliers, with Blackwell on deck to ramp significantly, sharing our in-depth research on stocks within the AI networking stack. We hold high allocations in two specific Nvidia AI networking suppliers.

Please note: The Tech Insider Network conducts research and draws conclusions for the company’s portfolio. We then share that information with our readers and offer real-time trade notifications. This is not a guarantee of a stock’s performance, and it is not financial advice. Please consult your personal financial advisor before buying any stock in the companies mentioned in this analysis. Beth Kindig and the Tech Insider Network own shares in NVDA at the time of writing, and may own stocks pictured in the charts.

data.title: Elanco Animal Health: What A Difference An Innovation Year Makes
data.description:
Back in May, I concluded that deleveraging takes time in the case of Elanco Animal Health (ELAN). The company and stock has been hurt by pipeline delays, debt incurred from the deal with Bayer and generally underwhelming growth, making that progress was painfully slow.

However, fast forwarding half a year in time, shares have risen some 80%, having risen from $12 to $22 per share. This move higher was driven by debt concerns moving definitively to the background, as the big driver is that the innovation part of the business is on fire. This has driven a well-deserved re-rating in the stock here.

Enriching Life With Animal Health

Elanco was the former animal health business from Eli Lilly (LLY) until that parent company decided to spin off this business already some 7 years ago, following the successful spin-off from Zoetis (ZTS) by Pfizer a couple of years before.

The company has a mission to help pets to live longer, healthier and more active lives. On the other item of the spectrum, the company helps farmers to improve animal health, wellbeing and raise livestock in a more sustainable manner.

For a long time, shares traded in a $20-$30 range, but this changed when the company acquired the Animal Health business from Bayer in a big deal. That transaction would add $1.7 billion in sales, with Elanco paying a 4.5 times sales multiples for these activities, being key to grow Elanco to the business which it currently is.

The promise was to create a business set to generate a billion in EBITDA, but that never materialized as the debt overhang created a real overhang in the years following, pressuring shares as well. This made that shares fell to the $10 mark in 2023, levels at which shares traded again earlier this year.

For the year 2024, the company generated $4.4 billion in sales with EBITDA seen just over $900 million, and while this missed the promises, these margins surpassed 20% of sales. Its activities, including over 200 brands, employ nearly 10,000 workers, serving customers in nearly 100 countries, covering over a dozen of manufacturing sites in its network as well. Revenues were split pretty evenly between pet health and livestock products, with the business having an even split between domestic and international activities.

Valuation Struggles

After the company grew 2021 sales to nearly $4.8 billion, revenues fell back to $4.4 billion in 2022 and 2023, with falling EBITDA metrics making that relative and absolute leverage was on the increase. Trying to tackle this, Elanco sold its aqua business last year in a $1.3 billion deal, for over a 7 times sales multiple, allowing the business to reduce net debt towards $4.5 billion, for leverage ratios still seen around 5 times EBITDA.

2024 sales rose by 0.5% to $4.44 billion, yet if we correct for the divestment of the aqua assets, revenues were up 3%. EBITDA was down 7% to $910 million, with adjusted earnings up two pennies to $0.91 per share. With net debt down to $3.9 billion and the company guiding for 2025 sales a midpoint of $4.48 billion, stability looked in sight. However, EBITDA was seen down to $850 million, hurt by the high-margins divestment of the aqua business.

However, there were more green shoots, as the company sold its royalties and commercial milestones related to XDEMVY in a $295 million cash deal, a useful amount to reduce debt further. Following a 4% increase in first quarter organic sales, the midpoint of the sales guidance was hiked to $4.545 billion, yet EBITDA and earnings estimates were kept intact.

Trading down to $12, the half a billion shares value equity of the business at $6 billion, for a $10 billion enterprise valuation. This is equal to about 2 times sales, a low-teens EBITDA multiple and 14-15 times earnings seen in the low-eighties cents per share. Still holding a modest long position, I felt hard-pressed to increase that position, but simultaneously saw no need to get out of the position as well.
